coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots with nitrogen and oxygen derived from simultaneous reaction of solvent as exfoliant and dopant
coredata.dc:creator: Kang, Gil-Seong
Lee, Sungho
Yeo, Jun-Seok
Choi, Eun-Su
Lee, Doh C.
Na, Seok-In
Joh, Han-Ik
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are promising materials for optoelectronic devices because their band-gap, derived from quantum confinement and edge effects, can be easily tuned via their size or surface/edge states. In this paper, a novel approach to synthesize nitrogen- and oxygen-doped GQDs (NO-GQDs) is presented. Nitrogen and oxygen are mainly bound at the GQD edges, resulting in high crystallinity and good electrical properties. A simple solvothermal reaction using N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), whose surface energy is similar to that of graphite as a raw material, can simultaneously exfoliate, cut, and finally transform the graphite into the GQDs with heteroatoms derived from the decomposed NMP solution. The synthesized NO-GQDs have a less defective and more selectively edge-functionalized structure compared to other reported GQDs. The electrical properties of NO-GQDs are investigated using them as the additive of hole-transporting materials (HTMs) in an optoelectronic device such as perovskite solar cells (PeSCs). Compared with PEDOT:PSS, a mixture of NO-GQDs and PEDOT:PSS shows a 36.2% increase in the power conversion efficiency (PCE) (maximum PCE: 11.47%) and good device stability. Therefore, it is believed that the improvement of photovoltaics is solely attributed from NO-GQDs which act as a positive role of faster hole transfer. We could confirm that the NO-GQDs facilitate hole-extraction from a photoactive layer and guarantee the more stable operation of PeSCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Nitrogen and oxygen doped graphene quantum dots (NO-GQDs)
Solvothermal reaction
Graphite exfoliation
Perovskite solar cells
Hole-transporting materials (HTMs)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by a grant from Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2018M1A2A2061989 and NRF-2018R1D1A1B07045368) and “Human Resources Program in Energy Technology” of the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP), granted financial resource from the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy, Republic of Korea. (No. 20174010201540 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of exfoliation and doping process for the NO-GQDs. (b) AFM image of the NO-GQDs and their heights (inset). (c) TEM image of the NO-GQDs and their size distribution (inset). (d) Closed TEM image and FFT patterns of a NO-GQDs (inset).
(a) XPS C1s spectra and (b) N1s spectra of the NO-GQDs. (c) GC–MS profiles of the NO-GQDs. (d) schematic illustration of the nitrogen and oxygen doping process in solvothermal reaction.
(a) UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence emission spectra of the NO-GQDs. (b) Photoluminescence emission spectra of the NO-GQDs, recorded from 325 nm to 400 nm.

(a) J-V curve of the devices with pristine and the NO-GQDs embedded. (b) EQE and IQE (inset) spectra of the devices. (c) Time-resolved decay curves of pristine PEDOT:PSS and the PEDOT + NO-GQDs. (d) Stability of the devices as a function of exposure time in atmosphere.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots with nitrogen and oxygen derived from simultaneous reaction of solvent as exfoliant and dopant

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Energy Engineering, Konkuk University, 120 Neungdong-ro, Gwangjin-gu, Seoul 05029, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are promising materials for optoelectronic devices because their band-gap, derived from quantum confinement and edge effects, can be easily tuned via their size or surface/edge states. In this paper, a novel approach to synthesize nitrogen- and oxygen-doped GQDs (NO-GQDs) is presented. Nitrogen and oxygen are mainly bound at the GQD edges, resulting in high crystallinity and good electrical properties. A simple solvothermal reaction using N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), whose surface energy is similar to that of graphite as a raw material, can simultaneously exfoliate, cut, and finally transform the graphite into the GQDs with heteroatoms derived from the decomposed NMP solution. The synthesized NO-GQDs have a less defective and more selectively edge-functionalized structure compared to other reported GQDs. The electrical properties of NO-GQDs are investigated using them as the additive of hole-transporting materials (HTMs) in an optoelectronic device such as perovskite solar cells (PeSCs). Compared with PEDOT:PSS, a mixture of NO-GQDs and PEDOT:PSS shows a 36.2% increase in the power conversion efficiency (PCE) (maximum PCE: 11.47%) and good device stability. Therefore, it is believed that the improvement of photovoltaics is solely attributed from NO-GQDs which act as a positive role of faster hole transfer. We could confirm that the NO-GQDs facilitate hole-extraction from a photoactive layer and guarantee the more stable operation of PeSCs.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and oxygen doped graphene quantum dots (NO-GQDs)

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are composed of two-dimensional (2D) few-layer graphene with nanoscale dimensions and have attracted considerable attention because of their exceptional properties of band-gap opening and fluorescent tuning in contrast to graphene sheets [1,2]. Precisely controlled GQDs have a finite band-gap because of quantum confinement and edge effects; thus, they are promising materials for various photonic and optoelectronic applications such as light emitting-diodes, biological imaging, and photovoltaics [3–7].

Numerous efforts to control the unique properties of GQDs, such as tunable band-gap and photoluminescence yield, have been reported. It is basically known that the properties of the GQDs can be tuned by size control and heteroatom doping [8–18]. In other words, the quantum confinement effect is highly related to the size or surface/edge state of GQDs. However, the size effect of GQDs is strictly restricted because of the infinite exciton Bohr radius of graphene compared to that of the metal-based quantum dots [19,20]. On the other hand, heteroatom doping can easily tune the band-gap and spectrum regions of GQDs from blue to red. Therefore, to control the properties of GQDs, the size and surface chemistry should be thoroughly considered from initial step to synthesize the GQDs.

Until now, various methods have been reported to form small-sized GQDs and incorporate heteroatoms into GQDs. These can be briefly summarized by two methods: top-down and bottom-up approaches. The top-down approach is a common method involving cutting bulk carbon materials, including graphite, coal, and carbon fibers, by using a strong acid [2,21,22]. The bottom-up approach is a chemical synthesis of hexagonal nanocarbon fragments from several organic precursors [11,23–28]. However, both approaches mostly produce low-quality GQDs that unavoidably contain structural defects, and this issue arose from following rationale. The top-down approach generally involves harsh oxidation steps to reduce the size, inherently generating permanent in-plane defects in GQDs. In addition, the synthesis of GQDs by bottom-up and some top-down approaches mostly involve the use of non-graphitic and amorphous carbon sources (e.g. coal, citric acid, glucose, and urea) as raw materials and an incomplete dehydration process for converting to CC, leading that CC bonds could not assemble the perfect hexagonal structure [29]. Therefore, the products resulting from these approaches are not GQDs but carbon nanodots (CNDs) or carbon dots (CDs), inevitably degrading their intrinsic optical and electrical properties.

Heteroatom doping effectively modulates the optical and electrical states of GQDs. Many researchers have reported on nitrogen-containing GQDs (N-GQDs) (or nitrogen-containing carbon materials) and their properties [30,31]. Yeh et al. reported on the band-gap configuration changes of nitrogen-doped graphene oxide quantum dots (N-GOQDs) compared to pristine GQDs [32]. Replacing the oxygen functional groups on the edge of graphene oxide (GO) sheets with nitrogen-containing groups transforms the orbitals and confines π-electrons in GQDs. Recently, Tetsuka et al. reported the tunable energy levels and optical properties of GQDs as a result of nitrogen functionalization on the edge, inducing an effective sp2 orbital resonance within the carbon atoms [33]. They also revealed that nitrogen doping of GQDs led to enhanced charge transport ability because of the long carrier lifetime of N-GQDs. However, the incorporation of N atoms into the graphene lattice through the above strategy is likely to disrupt the sp2 hybridization of C atoms and even induce structural defects in GQDs, which possibly hinders their chemical stability as well as electrical and/or optical properties [29,34]. However, doping in both hexagonal and defect sites cannot be avoided because the starting material, GO, contains defective structures. Furthermore, chemical reduction processes, which are generally used for heteroatom doping in oxygenated carbon materials, give rise to a number of permanent defects in GQDs, where oxygen functional groups are detached. Therefore, a systematic study on the development of edge N-functionalized GQDs with high crystallinity and outstanding intrinsic properties is necessary.

Here, we proposed a novel one-pot reaction for producing edge-N-/O-functionalized GQDs (NO-GQDs) with high crystallinity and electrical properties using graphite as the raw material. A simple solvothermal reaction using N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), which has surface energy similar to graphite, can realize NO-GQDs through a simultaneous process of exfoliation, scission and finally transform the graphite into the GQDs with heteroatoms derived from the decomposed NMP solution. The synthesized NO-GQDs have a less defective and more selectively edge-functionalized structure compared to other reported GQDs. To investigate the electrical properties of NO-GQDs, we applied them to the additive of hole-transporting materials (HTMs) in an optoelectronic device, i.e., a perovskite solar cell (PeSC). We confirmed that NO-GQDs facilitated hole-extraction from the photoactive layer and guaranteed a more stable operation of PeSCs.

Raw graphite (0.1 g) was dispersed in NMP (N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone, 70 ml) via ultrasonic treatment for 1 h. For the hydrothermal process, the mixture was put in a 150 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and elevated temperature to 300 °C and maintained for 24 h [35]. The resultant solution was filtered to remove the non-exfoliated graphite by using a 200-nm mesh filter. The NO-GQDs powder was obtained by heating the solution at 170 °C for 5 h; the powder was then dried in a N2 furnace (400 °C for 6 h) to completely remove the NMP solvent.

Powder NO-GQDs were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (K-alpha, Thermo Scientific), atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Dimension 3100, Veeco), UV–vis spectroscopy (V-570, Jasco), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F20 460 L), Raman spectroscopy (Horiba), FT-IR spectroscopy (Nicolet iS 10), and 1-dimensional X-ray diffraction (SmarLab). The well-dispersed NO-GQDs were characterized by photoluminescence (PL) (Horiba).

The PeSC device structure with NO-GQDs or PEDOT:PSS-based HTMs is illustrated in Fig. 4(a). The PeSC structure was composed of indium tin oxide (ITO)/HTM/CH3NH3PbI3 (perovskite)/PCBM/BCP/Ag. The bottom ITO substrate was cleaned using acetone, conventional detergent, distilled water, and isopropyl alcohol and then UV/O3 treated for 30 min. Then, the NO-GQDs-based HTMs were spin-coated onto the ITO substrates at 4000 rpm for 40 s, followed by film drying at 150 °C for 10 min. A conventional PEDOT:PSS (Clevios PVPAI 4083) device, which was used as the reference HTM, was also fabricated by spin coating at 4000 rpm for 40 s, followed by heat treatment at 150 °C for 10 min. For the deposition of CH3NH3PbI3 i.e., the light-harvesting layer, the precursor solution (35 wt%) was fabricated by first dissolving methylammonium iodide (MAI) and PbI2 with a 1:1 molar ratio (0.262 g: 0.759 g) in anhydrous N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) (2 mL) [36]; then N-cyclohexyl-2-pyrrolidone (CHP) (5 vol%), which is a morphology controller, was added into the mixture [37]. The mixture was spin coated at 6000 rpm for 90 s, followed by heat treatment at 100 °C for 5 min in ambient N2. Then, a PCBM (Nano-C) solution (0.02 g cm−3) in chlorobenzene was spin-coated at 1000 rpm for 60 s in N2. Finally, BCP (3 nm) and Ag (thickness of 80 nm) cathodes with an active area of 4.64 mm2 were thermally deposited onto the PCBM layer under vacuum at 10−6 Torr. The photocurrent density-voltage curves were obtained using a Keithley 2400 SMU instrument and an Oriel solar simulator (Class AAA) (100 mW cm−2, AM 1.5 G condition). The light intensity was calibrated using a standard Si cell that was certified by the International System of Units (SI) (SRC 1000 TC KG5 N, VLSI Standards, Inc). The EQE/IQE spectra were investigated using an Oriel® IQE-200 quantum efficiency (QE) measurement system and the PeSC lifetime was recorded as a function of exposure time to ambient air/humidity/temperature without encapsulation [38].

The schematic procedure for the one-step synthesis of heteroatom doped-GQDs is shown in Fig. 1 (a). Recent theoretical works demonstrated that the surface tension of a solvent for graphite exfoliation should be between 40 and 50 mJ m−2 (Table S1) [39,40]. Among various solvents, NMP was reported to have the most appropriate surface energy for the effective exfoliation of graphite. It is evident that NMP droplets exhibit high affinity with graphite with a low contact angle of 28.2°, as shown in Fig. S1, allowing the intercalation of NMP molecules into the graphite layers. When the temperature of a mixture containing graphite and NMP is elevated above the boiling point of NMP under a solvothermal condition, graphite starts to be exfoliated from the edges of its sheets by vapor pressure of the intercalated NMP molecules and is consecutively converted to NO-GQDs. After the formation of NO-GQDs, a noticeable and stable color change of NMP from transparent to brown was observed, implying that the exfoliated NO-GQDs were well-dispersed in the solution with high colloidal stability (Fig. S2).

As shown in Fig. 1(b) and (c), atomic force microscopy (AFM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images confirmed that NO-GQDs were well exfoliated from graphite and had well-defined coin shapes with an aspect ratio of approximately 10. The heights of NO-GQDs obtained by AFM ranged from 0.5 to 1.0 nm, indicating that the as-synthesized NO-GQDs consisted of 1–2 stacked graphene layers. The size distribution of NO-GQDs was relatively narrow with lateral diameters of 6–16 nm. More notably, a high-resolution TEM image and the corresponding fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern (Fig. 1(d)) showed that NO-GQDs have highly crystalline structures, which shows the distinct (100) lattice fringes of the sp2 graphitic carbon with a lattice parameter of 0.21 nm [32].

The crystalline structure of NO-GQDs was further verified by Raman spectroscopy and X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. S3, the Raman spectrum of NO-GQDs was characterized by the D and G bands at 1350 and 1568 cm−1, respectively. An integrated intensity ratio of the D band to the G band (ID/IG ) for NO-GQDs was found to be 0.90, which reveals the highly crystalline structure of NO-GQDs compared to that of the GQDs prepared by other synthetic approaches (Table S2) [1,2,21,22,41,42]. The XRD diffractogram confirms that NO-GQDs contained few-layered graphene structures, showing an interlayer spacing of 3.48 Å, which is larger than that of as-received graphite (3.354 Å), as shown in Fig. S4. This slight difference in interlayer spacing may result from mechanical deformation, such as compression and decompression of graphite, in the exfoliation step and/or edge functionalization by the nitrogen and oxygen species [24].

In order to confirm the doping of heteroatoms on NO-GQDs, Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were carried out. Fig. S5(a) shows the nitrogen and oxygen functional groups such as NH2 and OH (broad peak at 3400 cm−1), CO (1689 cm−1), CN (1542 cm−1), and CO (1249 cm−1) in NO-GQDs. In addition, relatively high elemental concentrations of N and O were also observed, i.e., 12 and 16 at.%, respectively, which is confirmed by the XPS survey spectrum of NO-GQDs (Fig. S5(b)) even though the NO-GQDs have a higher crystallinity compared to the other GQDs. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), the deconvoluted C 1 s spectrum revealed the presence of CC (284.7 eV), CN (285.6 eV), CO (286.6 eV), CO (288.2 eV), and COOH (289.5 eV) bonds in NO-GQDs. Nitrogen atoms were incorporated into GQDs and well-bonded in graphitic structures, forming pyridinic (398.0 eV), pyrrolic (399.9 eV), and oxidized N (402.9 eV), as shown in Fig. 2(b). By comparing the relative intensities of the deconvoluted N 1s peaks, it is confirmed that most of the nitrogen components consist of pyrrolic and pyridinic states, which is likely to exist on the edges of the graphitic planes. These FT-IR and XPS results clearly confirm that the heteroatoms of oxygen and nitrogen were effectively doped at the edges of the GQDs exfoliated from graphite without any chemical additives.

It is speculated that NMP could supply N and O atoms for binding with C atoms during the synthesis of NO-GQDs. The doping mechanism could be partly clarified by gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC/MS) of colloidal NO-GQDs in NMP, as shown in Fig. 2(c). The peak labeled 2, which is assigned to the NMP molecules, is the most dominant. Interestingly, peaks corresponding to the decomposed NMP molecules are clearly observed around the NMP peak (the peak labeled 3, 4); these peaks are assigned to N- and O-containing molecules such as N-methyl-formamide, N-methyl-propanamide, acetamide, and propanamide. It is believed that the NMP molecules were decomposed to the aforementioned structures under the hydrothermal conditions and were spontaneously incorporated into the defective edges of NO-GQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 2(d). This indicates that NMP can simultaneously act as an exfoliator and a dopant for NO-GQDs.

To explore the characteristics of the NO-GQDs derived from graphite and NMP, optical absorption and photoluminescence (PL) of colloidal NO-GQDs were examined, as shown in Fig. 3 . In the UV–vis absorption spectrum, two absorption peaks are observed. The absorption bands at 273 and 322 nm are assigned to the π → π* transition of aromatic sp2 structures and the n → π* transition of oxidized or heteroatom-doped properties related to CN and CO, respectively [43–48]. The colloidal NO-GQDs solution exhibited a PL emission with the maximum peak at 455 nm (λex = 360 nm), indicating that NO-GQDs have strong quantum confinement. The inset in Fig. 3(a) is a photographic image of the colloidal NO-GQDs solution obtained with an excitation laser of 365 nm, showing a clear sky blue color. Upon increasing the excitation wavelength, we observed that the PL emission was significantly enhanced and red-shifted; thus, an excitation of 400 nm resulted in an intense PL emission centered at ∼500 nm (Fig. 3(b)). These results imply that the PL emission and fluorescence of NO-GQDs mostly stem from n → π* transitions of the CO and CN groups [24]. In general, the GQDs synthesized by the top-down approach suffer from a low quantum yield because the intrinsic π → π* transition results in significantly low efficiency for electron excitation and emission. However, interestingly, the proposed NO-GQDs revealed a rather high quantum yield of 19.1% due to the surface passivation of NO-GQDs by pyrrolic N functional groups [44,46–48].

The high crystallinity and heteroatomic composition of NO-GQDs enable us to explore their feasibility as the additive of HTMs in optoelectronic devices [49,50]. We fabricated planar PeSCs with a configuration of glass/ITO/HTM/CH3NH3PbI3/[6,6]-phenyl C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PCBM)/bathocuproine (BCP)/Ag. The representative photocurrent density-voltage (J-V) curves and statistical data of the photovoltaic parameters are presented in Figs. 4 (a) and S6, respectively.

The control device using PEDOT:PSS HTM exhibited an open-circuit voltage (VOC ) of 0.81 V, a short-circuit current (JSC ) of 12.30 mA cm−2, a fill factor (FF) of 80.94%, and a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 8.04% (max. 8.42%). The incorporation of 10 vol% NO-GQDs in PEDOT:PSS (PEDOT + NO-GQDs) resulted in a PCE increment of 36.2% compared to that of a pristine PEDOT:PSS device with the following properties: VOC of 0.80 V, JSC of 15.37 mA cm−2, FF of 82.65%, and PCE of 10.15% (max. 11.47%). It is noticeable that JSC and FF are the main factors responsible for the enhanced performance of PeSCs with PEDOT + NO-GQDs. The external quantum efficiency (EQE) of PeSCs using the PEDOT + NO-GQDs showed higher values over the entire range of 300–800 nm compared to the PEDOT:PSS device, as shown in Fig. 4(b). The enhancement of EQE is almost equal to that of the internal quantum efficiency (IQE), which indicates that there are negligible differences in the absorption efficiencies between the PEDOT + NO-GQDs and the PEDOT:PSS devices. Accordingly, the enhanced EQE mainly resulted from the increased IQE, which is directly related to charge-transport and charge-collection in PeSCs, leading to the improved JSC and FF of the NO-GQDs-containing devices. To unravel the effects of NO-GQDs on charge extraction in perovskite devices, we examined the time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra of a perovskite layer grown on pristine PEDOT:PSS and PEDOT + NO-GQDs (Fig. 4(c)). It was observed that the PL decay time of the perovskite layer on PEDOT + NO-GQDs (7.01 ns) was much lower than that of the perovskite layer on pristine PEDOT:PSS HTM (16.79 ns). This indicates that NO-GQDs could facilitate the extraction of photogenerated charge carriers within the perovskite layer without considerable charge recombination. This enhancement probably originated from the high electrical conductivity of NO-GQDs and the crystal-defect passivation of perovskite by the nitrogen functional groups in NO-GQDs [25,51,52].

To further elucidate the effectiveness of NO-GQDs, the operation stability of the PeSCs using NO-GQDs-containing HTMs was tested according to the ISOS-D-1 protocol. As shown in Fig. 4(d), the PCEs of the PEDOT:PSS and PEDOT + NO-GQDs devices were recorded and compared as a function of exposure time to ambient conditions. The PCEs of the PEDOT:PSS and PEDOT:PSS + NO-GQDs devices were degraded rapidly up to 80 h. The accurately normalized PCEs of PEDOT:PSS and PEDOT + NO-GQDs PSCs after 80 h were 0.43 and 0.52 (In the case of a PCE, the former and the latter were decreased from 8.44 to 3.65 % and from 10.31 to 5.37 %, respectively, as shown in Fig. 4d (inset)), respectively, resulted in a PCE increment of 20.9 % as incorporation of NO-GQDs into PEDOT:PSS. Further, in the case of the PEDOT:PSS device, the photovoltaic performance was dramatically degraded and completely failed after exposure of 440 h. In contrast, even after an exposure time of 600 h, the PeSC with PEDOT + NO-GQDs maintained its photovoltaic characteristics. This indicated that the NO-GQDs were attributed to the enhanced stability despite of only 10 % blending. We believed that NO-GQDs were acted as an oxygen barrier.

In conclusion, we developed a novel method to produce high-quality heteroatomic NO-GQDs using graphite and NMP as source materials. Through a hydrothermal process, the intercalation and decomposition of NMP molecules into graphite enabled NMP to function as the exfoliator and the dopant. The resulting NO-GQDs exhibited high quantum yield due to their well-confined structures and the presence of N-/O- functional groups. Furthermore, by incorporating NO-GQDs as the additive of HTMs into PeSCs, a 36.2% enhancement in the PCE was achieved, compared to a pristine PEDOT:PSS device, mostly because of facilitated charge extraction through NO-GQDs/perovskite interfaces. This simple solvent-derived process is scalable, versatile, and effective for paving the way to large production of these materials.

This work was supported by a grant from Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2018M1A2A2061989 and NRF-2018R1D1A1B07045368) and “Human Resources Program in Energy Technology” of the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP), granted financial resource from the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy, Republic of Korea. (No. 20174010201540).
The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2019.04.192.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Mass production of graphene quantum dots by one- pot synthesis directly from graphite in high yield

Size-controllable and low-cost fabrication of graphene quantum dots using thermal plasma jet
Gram-scale synthesis of single-crystalline graphene quantum dots with superior optical properties
